title: Ankle Fracture
oneLiner: Fracture of distal tibia/fibula from trauma; common in falls & twists; risk of joint incongruity / non-union / compartment syndrome.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Open fracture (any wound over fracture site)** – the single finding is **visible bone or fat globules**, or a wound that tracks to bone. Risk of osteomyelitis / sepsis → needs immediate antibiotics + wound washout.
- **Vascular compromise** – **absent dorsalis pedis / posterior tibial pulse** after gentle reduction. Ankle dislocation with fracture can occlude vessels → urgent closed reduction + vascular opinion if not restored.
- **Compartment syndrome** (evolves over hours, not first minute) – **pain out of proportion** + pain on passive toe flexion → check compartment pressure if suspected; measure serum CK.
sections.Presentation:
- **Mechanism:** twisting (inversion/eversion) or axial loading (fall from height, MVA). Often heard “crack” and immediate pain.
- **Symptoms:** inability to weight bear immediately, marked swelling, deformity (if fracture-dislocation), ecchymosis.
- **Exam findings:**  
  - Tenderness over medial / lateral malleolus, syndesmosis, or proximal fibula.  
  - Swelling ± blistering.  
  - Deformity: foot externally rotated / shortened in dislocation.  
  - **Always document neurovascular status** (sensation webspace, posterior tibial/dorsalis pedis pulses, motor: toe flex/extend) – both before & after any manipulation.
  - Skin integrity: any open wound? (assume open until proven – saline test not reliable; formal wound exploration by orthopaedics).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Ankle sprain** (ligament injury without fracture) – able to bear weight? No bony tenderness? Ottawa rules negative. **But** sprain can coexist with fracture – XR essential.
- **Syndesmotic injury** (high ankle sprain) – pain at syndesmosis, positive squeeze test / external rotation test. Often with fracture (Maisonneuve).
- **Talus fracture / dislocation** – pain over talar dome, anterior ankle tenderness; may be missed on XR if not scrutinised.
- **Peroneal tendon dislocation** – painful snapping sensation; visible peroneal tendon on dynamic US.
- **Lisfranc injury** (midfoot fracture-dislocation) – plantar ecchymosis, inability to stand on toes; need foot XR.
- **Splitter:** ability to bear weight (or even partial) *immediately* after injury strongly favours sprain over fracture – but not 100%. Ottawa rules are still the best bedside splitter.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - **Ottawa Ankle Rules** (walk 4 steps, bony tenderness over posterior edge of malleoli or base of 5th metatarsal / navicular) – if negative, no XR needed in ED.  
  - Neurovascular check (do before & after reduction).  
  - Apply + check backslab / splint.
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, clotting – if open fracture or planned surgery.  
  - Group & save if ORIF likely.  
  - Creatine kinase (CK) if compartment syndrome suspected.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First line:** Weight-bearing? Usually non-weight-bearing if fracture suspected – but **XR ankle (mortise, AP, lateral)** + if negative but high suspicion, XR foot (to rule out base of 5th metatarsal/ navicular fracture).  
  - **Second line:** CT ankle if complex fracture pattern / involvement of tibial plafond (pilon fracture). MRI if occult fracture / osteochondral lesion suspected.  
  - **Never-miss test:** **XR of whole tibia/fibula if fibular fracture or medial tenderness** – to rule out **Maisonneuve fracture** (proximal fibula fracture with syndesmotic injury + medial malleolus fracture or deltoid ligament rupture).  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - **Weber classification** (A, B, C) based on level of fibular fracture relative to syndesmosis – determines stability & need for surgery.  
  - **Lauge-Hansen classification** (e.g. supination-external rotation, pronation-abduction) – more detailed but less commonly used acutely.  
  - **Open fractures:** Gustilo-Anderson classification (I, II, III) – dictates antibiotic duration and urgency.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Reduce dislocation promptly (traction + countertraction; then moulded backslab).  
  - Apply backslab / Plaster of Paris (POP) posterior splint with “U” slab - ensure well-padded, not tight.  
  - Elevate limb on pillow.  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO/IV, consider ibuprofen 400mg PO if no contraindications, severe: morphine or fentanyl IV [check local policy for dose].  
  - **If open fracture:**  
    - Cover wound with sterile saline-soaked gauze.  
    - IV antibiotics: co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS (or cefuroxime ± metronidazole) – [check local policy].  
    - Tetanus status – human tetanus immunoglobulin if high risk / non-immunised.  
    - Urgent orthopaedic review for washout and stabilisation.  
  - NBM if planned for theatre.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue elevation, ice packs (20 min on/off).  
  - Monitor neurovascular status 4-hourly for first 24h.  
  - Pain control: regular paracetamol + NSAIDs (if no contraindications).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin [check local dose] (e.g. 40mg SC daily) if immobilised / non-weight-bearing / surgery planned.  
  - For open fractures: review wound status daily; continue antibiotics for 24h post-washout (total course 24-48h unless signs of infection).  
  - If closed unimalleolar fracture with <2mm displacement & no syndesmotic injury: consider non-operative management with cast (backslab → full cast at 2 weeks) – may be discharged with fracture clinic follow-up.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Fracture clinic review within 2 weeks (ideally 5-7 days for first check).  
  - Weight-bearing status: non-weight-bearing for 6-8 weeks if unstable fracture / ORIF.  
  - Physiotherapy: range-of-motion exercises from week 1 if stable; strength training after union.  
  - Hardware removal: rarely needed unless symptomatic.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IVI if NBM – Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl (≤2L/day unless dehydrated).  
  - VTE: Enoxaparin [check local dose] – start at admission if not already on therapeutic anticoagulation.  
  - Diet: NBM if planned for ORIF; otherwise free (but encourage high protein/low sugar).  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (or as per hospital protocol – more frequent if cast syndrome suspected? Rare).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS (max 4g/24h).  
  - Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (max 1200mg/day) – avoid if GFR <30, asthma, GI bleed risk.  
  - Morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (for severe pain) – titrate; monitor sedation.  
  - Enoxaparin [check local dose] – e.g. 40mg SC OD for VTE prophylaxis in surgical patients.  
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS (open fracture) – [check local guidelines; alternative: cefazolin ± metronidazole].  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Codeine 30-60mg PO QDS (added to paracetamol) – use caution in elderly.  
  - Tramadol 50-100mg PO QDS (if opioid needed but less respiratory depression).  
  - Dalteparin / Tinzaparin [check local dose] for VTE.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid in asthma, renal impairment, GI bleeding, concomitant anticoagulation.  
  - Opioids: avoid in respiratory depression, opioid-naive use with caution.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin: dose adjust if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g. 20mg SC OD).  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if GFR <30.  
  - Morphine: reduce dose or interval if hepatic impairment / CKD.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Enoxaparin** – omitting it in non-weight-bearing patients (VTE risk high) or giving incorrect dose in renal impairment (bleeding risk).  
  - **Co-amoxiclav** – failing to adjust for renal impairment (double dose interval if CrCl <30).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Compartment syndrome – pain out of proportion, tense swelling, pain on passive toe extension, paraesthesiae; early fasciotomy.  
  - Wound infection / dehiscence (open fractures).  
  - VTE (DVT/PE) – VTE prophylaxis essential.  
  - Cast / backslab pressure sores – ask about pain under cast.  
  - Neurovascular injury: superficial peroneal nerve (foot drop? Sensory loss on dorsum); posterior tibial artery.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Post-traumatic osteoarthritis (especially if joint incongruity >2mm).  
  - Malunion / non-union (risk factors: smoking, diabetes, infection).  
  - Ankle stiffness / chronic pain – physiotherapy and orthopaedic review for secondary arthritis.  
  - Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) – disproportionate pain, sweating, colour change; early referral to pain team.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** Very low from isolated ankle fracture (high-energy + multiple injuries → 1-2% in-hospital mortality due to trauma / complications).  
- **Recovery:** Most unimalleolar fractures heal in 6-8 weeks; return to sport at 3-6 months. Up to 20% develop radiological arthritis, but only half symptomatic.  
- **Chronicity:** 1 in 3 patients have some residual pain or stiffness at 1 year, but most return to daily activities.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check entire tibia** in any medial malleolus or ankle fracture – a high fibula fracture (Maisonneuve) means syndesmotic injury requiring ORIF, not just ankle cast.  
- **Document neurovascular exam before and after every reduction** – this is medicolegal and guides emergency management.  
- **Use Ottawa rules to reduce unnecessary XR** in low-risk presentations – but remember if >55 years, auto-positive (any weight-bearing difficulty warrants XR).  
- **Elevate the foot above heart level** in ward – reduces swelling, delays blistering, allows earlier surgery.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never manipulate a fracture-dislocation without sedation or adequate analgesia** – poor reduction, iatrogenic injury, patient distress.  
- **Do not discharge a displaced bimalleolar / trimalleolar fracture without orthopaedic review** – these require ORIF within 24-48h; delayed surgery increases complications.  
- **Avoid using NSAIDs in patients with history of GI bleeds or CKD** – prescribe paracetamol + codeine instead.  
- **Do not ignore pain under a cast** – this is compartment syndrome or pressure sore until proven otherwise; remove cast immediately.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - **Severe pain** unrelieved by analgesia, or pain that feels worse over time.  
  - **Numbness, tingling, or colour change** in toes / foot – arterial compromise.  
  - **Wound discharge, redness, fever** – infection (open fractures).  
  - **Chest pain / shortness of breath** – PE (rare but possible).  
- **Follow-up:** Fracture clinic in 7-10 days for repeat XR / clinical review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or **NEWS2 ≥3 with one red parameter** → call the responsible team / rapid response.  
- **Compartment syndrome** (suspected: pain out of proportion, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesiae, paralysis) → immediate orthopaedic referral (“fasciotomy needed now”).  
- **Open fracture** (suspected or confirmed) → inform orthopaedic registrar for urgent washout (within 12 hours).  
- **Vascular compromise** (absent pulse after reduction) → call senior orthopaedic + vascular surgical team (critical limb ischaemia).  
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** or **base deficit < -6** in trauma patient → consider massive transfusion / ICU involvement.

rawMarkdown:
# Ankle Fracture
**One-liner:** Fracture of distal tibia/fibula from trauma; common in falls & twists; risk of joint incongruity / non-union / compartment syndrome.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Open fracture (any wound over fracture site)** – the single finding is **visible bone or fat globules**, or a wound that tracks to bone. Risk of osteomyelitis / sepsis → needs immediate antibiotics + wound washout.
- **Vascular compromise** – **absent dorsalis pedis / posterior tibial pulse** after gentle reduction. Ankle dislocation with fracture can occlude vessels → urgent closed reduction + vascular opinion if not restored.
- **Compartment syndrome** (evolves over hours, not first minute) – **pain out of proportion** + pain on passive toe flexion → check compartment pressure if suspected; measure serum CK.

### Presentation
- **Mechanism:** twisting (inversion/eversion) or axial loading (fall from height, MVA). Often heard “crack” and immediate pain.
- **Symptoms:** inability to weight bear immediately, marked swelling, deformity (if fracture-dislocation), ecchymosis.
- **Exam findings:**  
  - Tenderness over medial / lateral malleolus, syndesmosis, or proximal fibula.  
  - Swelling ± blistering.  
  - Deformity: foot externally rotated / shortened in dislocation.  
  - **Always document neurovascular status** (sensation webspace, posterior tibial/dorsalis pedis pulses, motor: toe flex/extend) – both before & after any manipulation.
  - Skin integrity: any open wound? (assume open until proven – saline test not reliable; formal wound exploration by orthopaedics).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Ankle sprain** (ligament injury without fracture) – able to bear weight? No bony tenderness? Ottawa rules negative. **But** sprain can coexist with fracture – XR essential.
- **Syndesmotic injury** (high ankle sprain) – pain at syndesmosis, positive squeeze test / external rotation test. Often with fracture (Maisonneuve).
- **Talus fracture / dislocation** – pain over talar dome, anterior ankle tenderness; may be missed on XR if not scrutinised.
- **Peroneal tendon dislocation** – painful snapping sensation; visible peroneal tendon on dynamic US.
- **Lisfranc injury** (midfoot fracture-dislocation) – plantar ecchymosis, inability to stand on toes; need foot XR.
- **Splitter:** ability to bear weight (or even partial) *immediately* after injury strongly favours sprain over fracture – but not 100%. Ottawa rules are still the best bedside splitter.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - **Ottawa Ankle Rules** (walk 4 steps, bony tenderness over posterior edge of malleoli or base of 5th metatarsal / navicular) – if negative, no XR needed in ED.  
  - Neurovascular check (do before & after reduction).  
  - Apply + check backslab / splint.
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, clotting – if open fracture or planned surgery.  
  - Group & save if ORIF likely.  
  - Creatine kinase (CK) if compartment syndrome suspected.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First line:** Weight-bearing? Usually non-weight-bearing if fracture suspected – but **XR ankle (mortise, AP, lateral)** + if negative but high suspicion, XR foot (to rule out base of 5th metatarsal/ navicular fracture).  
  - **Second line:** CT ankle if complex fracture pattern / involvement of tibial plafond (pilon fracture). MRI if occult fracture / osteochondral lesion suspected.  
  - **Never-miss test:** **XR of whole tibia/fibula if fibular fracture or medial tenderness** – to rule out **Maisonneuve fracture** (proximal fibula fracture with syndesmotic injury + medial malleolus fracture or deltoid ligament rupture).  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - **Weber classification** (A, B, C) based on level of fibular fracture relative to syndesmosis – determines stability & need for surgery.  
  - **Lauge-Hansen classification** (e.g. supination-external rotation, pronation-abduction) – more detailed but less commonly used acutely.  
  - **Open fractures:** Gustilo-Anderson classification (I, II, III) – dictates antibiotic duration and urgency.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Reduce dislocation promptly (traction + countertraction; then moulded backslab).  
  - Apply backslab / Plaster of Paris (POP) posterior splint with “U” slab - ensure well-padded, not tight.  
  - Elevate limb on pillow.  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO/IV, consider ibuprofen 400mg PO if no contraindications, severe: morphine or fentanyl IV [check local policy for dose].  
  - **If open fracture:**  
    - Cover wound with sterile saline-soaked gauze.  
    - IV antibiotics: co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS (or cefuroxime ± metronidazole) – [check local policy].  
    - Tetanus status – human tetanus immunoglobulin if high risk / non-immunised.  
    - Urgent orthopaedic review for washout and stabilisation.  
  - NBM if planned for theatre.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue elevation, ice packs (20 min on/off).  
  - Monitor neurovascular status 4-hourly for first 24h.  
  - Pain control: regular paracetamol + NSAIDs (if no contraindications).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin [check local dose] (e.g. 40mg SC daily) if immobilised / non-weight-bearing / surgery planned.  
  - For open fractures: review wound status daily; continue antibiotics for 24h post-washout (total course 24-48h unless signs of infection).  
  - If closed unimalleolar fracture with <2mm displacement & no syndesmotic injury: consider non-operative management with cast (backslab → full cast at 2 weeks) – may be discharged with fracture clinic follow-up.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Fracture clinic review within 2 weeks (ideally 5-7 days for first check).  
  - Weight-bearing status: non-weight-bearing for 6-8 weeks if unstable fracture / ORIF.  
  - Physiotherapy: range-of-motion exercises from week 1 if stable; strength training after union.  
  - Hardware removal: rarely needed unless symptomatic.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IVI if NBM – Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl (≤2L/day unless dehydrated).  
  - VTE: Enoxaparin [check local dose] – start at admission if not already on therapeutic anticoagulation.  
  - Diet: NBM if planned for ORIF; otherwise free (but encourage high protein/low sugar).  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (or as per hospital protocol – more frequent if cast syndrome suspected? Rare).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS (max 4g/24h).  
  - Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (max 1200mg/day) – avoid if GFR <30, asthma, GI bleed risk.  
  - Morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (for severe pain) – titrate; monitor sedation.  
  - Enoxaparin [check local dose] – e.g. 40mg SC OD for VTE prophylaxis in surgical patients.  
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS (open fracture) – [check local guidelines; alternative: cefazolin ± metronidazole].  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Codeine 30-60mg PO QDS (added to paracetamol) – use caution in elderly.  
  - Tramadol 50-100mg PO QDS (if opioid needed but less respiratory depression).  
  - Dalteparin / Tinzaparin [check local dose] for VTE.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid in asthma, renal impairment, GI bleeding, concomitant anticoagulation.  
  - Opioids: avoid in respiratory depression, opioid-naive use with caution.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin: dose adjust if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g. 20mg SC OD).  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if GFR <30.  
  - Morphine: reduce dose or interval if hepatic impairment / CKD.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Enoxaparin** – omitting it in non-weight-bearing patients (VTE risk high) or giving incorrect dose in renal impairment (bleeding risk).  
  - **Co-amoxiclav** – failing to adjust for renal impairment (double dose interval if CrCl <30).  

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Compartment syndrome – pain out of proportion, tense swelling, pain on passive toe extension, paraesthesiae; early fasciotomy.  
  - Wound infection / dehiscence (open fractures).  
  - VTE (DVT/PE) – VTE prophylaxis essential.  
  - Cast / backslab pressure sores – ask about pain under cast.  
  - Neurovascular injury: superficial peroneal nerve (foot drop? Sensory loss on dorsum); posterior tibial artery.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Post-traumatic osteoarthritis (especially if joint incongruity >2mm).  
  - Malunion / non-union (risk factors: smoking, diabetes, infection).  
  - Ankle stiffness / chronic pain – physiotherapy and orthopaedic review for secondary arthritis.  
  - Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) – disproportionate pain, sweating, colour change; early referral to pain team.

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** Very low from isolated ankle fracture (high-energy + multiple injuries → 1-2% in-hospital mortality due to trauma / complications).  
- **Recovery:** Most unimalleolar fractures heal in 6-8 weeks; return to sport at 3-6 months. Up to 20% develop radiological arthritis, but only half symptomatic.  
- **Chronicity:** 1 in 3 patients have some residual pain or stiffness at 1 year, but most return to daily activities.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check entire tibia** in any medial malleolus or ankle fracture – a high fibula fracture (Maisonneuve) means syndesmotic injury requiring ORIF, not just ankle cast.  
- **Document neurovascular exam before and after every reduction** – this is medicolegal and guides emergency management.  
- **Use Ottawa rules to reduce unnecessary XR** in low-risk presentations – but remember if >55 years, auto-positive (any weight-bearing difficulty warrants XR).  
- **Elevate the foot above heart level** in ward – reduces swelling, delays blistering, allows earlier surgery.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never manipulate a fracture-dislocation without sedation or adequate analgesia** – poor reduction, iatrogenic injury, patient distress.  
- **Do not discharge a displaced bimalleolar / trimalleolar fracture without orthopaedic review** – these require ORIF within 24-48h; delayed surgery increases complications.  
- **Avoid using NSAIDs in patients with history of GI bleeds or CKD** – prescribe paracetamol + codeine instead.  
- **Do not ignore pain under a cast** – this is compartment syndrome or pressure sore until proven otherwise; remove cast immediately.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - **Severe pain** unrelieved by analgesia, or pain that feels worse over time.  
  - **Numbness, tingling, or colour change** in toes / foot – arterial compromise.  
  - **Wound discharge, redness, fever** – infection (open fractures).  
  - **Chest pain / shortness of breath** – PE (rare but possible).  
- **Follow-up:** Fracture clinic in 7-10 days for repeat XR / clinical review.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or **NEWS2 ≥3 with one red parameter** → call the responsible team / rapid response.  
- **Compartment syndrome** (suspected: pain out of proportion, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesiae, paralysis) → immediate orthopaedic referral (“fasciotomy needed now”).  
- **Open fracture** (suspected or confirmed) → inform orthopaedic registrar for urgent washout (within 12 hours).  
- **Vascular compromise** (absent pulse after reduction) → call senior orthopaedic + vascular surgical team (critical limb ischaemia).  
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** or **base deficit < -6** in trauma patient → consider massive transfusion / ICU involvement.
